title: ActProof Events catalogue entry, v3
description: Schema for a single catalogue entry describing a regulated or governance act type. v3 is a strict additive superset of v2. It adds six optional blocks for richer act-type semantics: regulated_context_profile (constrains receipt context shapes), prior_receipts_profile (declares bilateral lifecycle expectations), reliance_context (names who relies and who verifies, and what the receipt does not prove), disclosure_profile (per-field disclosure tier declarations and back-propagation scope), submission_evidence_policy (declares recognised submission and transmission evidence artifacts), and claim_field_types (declares the primitive data type of each claim field, so catalogue-driven consumers need no hardcoded field-type knowledge). All sixteen required v2 fields are unchanged. Any v2-shaped entry validates against v3 with only its schema discriminator updated to 'actproof.act_profile.v3'. The v2 schema file remains in the repository as a historical record for consumers pinned to v2. submission_evidence_policy, claim_field_types, and the optional reliance_context.non_claims field were introduced in additive revisions of v3, alongside additional controlled-vocabulary values. Because these changes are optional or additive, entries authored before them remain valid. The source_bindings, generation, and transparency_note_reference fields were introduced in a further additive revision: source_bindings pins the official legal instruments a profile is bound to, generation records how the profile was produced, and transparency_note_reference points to its human-readable transparency note. All three are optional, so entries authored before them remain valid.

schema.description: Schema discriminator. Always the literal string above. v3 entries are recognised by actproof-py v0.1.1 and later. actproof-py v0.1.0 and earlier accept only v2 entries.

act_type_id.description: Canonical identifier under the op: namespace. Dotted segments terminate with a version suffix. Example: op:eu.nis2.art20.management_body_approval.v1

claim_type.description: Snake_case identifier describing the semantic shape of the claim. Examples: management_body_approval, due_diligence_statement_preparation, software_release.

display_name.description: Human-readable display name suitable for UI surfaces and documentation.

instrument.description: Name of the regulatory instrument. Example: Directive (EU) 2022/2555.

article.description: Article, section, or paragraph reference within the instrument.

jurisdiction.description: ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code, ISO 3166-2 subdivision code, or supranational tag (EU, UN).

regulatory_citation.description: Regulatory anchor for the act type. null for non-regulatory governance acts (e.g., open-source software releases, internal corporate acts without a binding regulatory citation).

required_claim_fields.description: Manifest field identifiers that MUST be present in any issued attestation against this act type. Catalogue loaders MUST reject manifests missing any required field.

optional_claim_fields.description: Manifest field identifiers that MAY be present in an issued attestation.

required_evidence_labels.description: Evidence label identifiers that MUST have at least one uploaded artifact each before an attestation can transition to the awaiting_commit state.

eligible_issuer_roles.description: Role identifiers indicating which entity types are eligible to issue this act. Examples: essential_entity, important_entity, eu_operator, eu_trader, open_source_maintainer.

recommended_witness_roles.description: Role identifiers indicating which parties typically receive verification handles for this act type. These are functional descriptions of recipients in relation to the issuing entity. Examples: external_auditor, competent_authority_supervisor, outside_counsel, internal_audit, do_insurer, downstream_buyer, supply_chain_finance_provider.

minimum.description: Floor of what must be present at commit time. issuer_record means the platform-recorded commit action (email, IP, user agent, single-use token hash, manifest hash, consent text version, timestamp) suffices as evidence of the issuer's commit action. external_signature requires an externally produced signature file (e.g., QES, AES, signed PDF) to be attached as evidence. either accepts both. CRITICAL: issuer_record is an evidence record only. It is NOT an Advanced Electronic Signature or Qualified Electronic Signature under Regulation (EU) 910/2014 (eIDAS). Implementations MUST NOT claim that the platform-recorded issuer action is itself an eIDAS-grade signature.

supports.description: Evidence label identifiers for externally produced signature artifacts the act type recognizes alongside issuer_record. Issuers who require eIDAS AES or QES MUST attach the corresponding artifact and reference it under one of these labels. in_toto_statement_v1 and slsa_provenance_v1_attestation are recognised for software_release act types and map onto the in-toto and SLSA Provenance v1 vocabularies respectively; they are not eIDAS signatures.

signature_policy.description: Signature evidence policy for this act type. Defines both the minimum acceptable commit action and the externally produced signature artifacts the act recognizes.

version.description: Monotonic version of this catalogue entry. Increment on any semantic change. The version is also encoded in the act_type_id suffix (e.g., .v1, .v2).

supersedes.description: act_type_id of the predecessor entry this version replaces. null if this is the first version of the act type. The supersedes pattern allows entries without explicit version suffixes for legacy v1 namespace preservation.

maintainer.description: Maintainer identifier (typically the project or working group responsible for curating this entry). Example: actproof-events.

test_vector_reference.description: Repository-relative path to the JSON file containing test vectors for this entry. Test vectors MUST use computed hashes (not synthetic placeholders) so verifier implementations can reproduce them.

allowed_context_types.description: Permitted values of receipt envelope regulated_context.context_type for receipts issued under this act type. regulatory_filing is used where the act records a filing, notification, submission, or prepared filing package directed to a competent authority, regulator, or other legally designated recipient.

allowed_submission_stages.description: Permitted values of receipt envelope regulated_context.submission_stage. The set MUST be coherent with allowed_context_types per the receipt envelope spec: each stage belongs to one of the allowed context types.

default_context_type.description: Default context_type assumed when an issuer does not supply one. MUST be a member of allowed_context_types.

regulated_context_profile.description: Restricts which regulated_context shapes are valid for receipts under this act type. Optional. Absent means any context shape permitted by the receipt envelope schema applies. Introduced in v3.

required_roles.description: Role identifiers that MUST appear in a receipt's prior_receipts array. Empty array or absent block means no prior receipts required.

optional_roles.description: Role identifiers that MAY appear in a receipt's prior_receipts array. Validators MAY reject prior_receipts entries whose role is not named in required_roles or optional_roles.

prior_receipts_profile.description: Declares the bilateral lifecycle expectations for receipts under this act type. Optional. Absent means no prior receipts expected and no restrictions on roles. Introduced in v3.

issuer_role.description: Functional description of the entity issuing receipts under this act type. Examples: 'open-source maintainer issuing a release attestation', 'EU operator preparing a Due Diligence Statement'.

counterparty_action.description: What the counterparty does on receiving the receipt. Examples: 'accepts the release for downstream packaging', 'relies on the statement for placing on the EU market', 'records the attestation for the audit trail'.

later_verifiers.description: Categories of third parties expected to verify the receipt independently at later points in time. Examples: 'regulator', 'external auditor', 'grant evaluator', 'downstream integrator', 'public'.

reliance_statement.description: One-sentence statement of what the receipt asserts that the counterparty and later verifiers may rely upon. Optional but recommended for any act type whose value depends on third-party reliance.

non_claims.description: Machine-readable identifiers for inferences that a verifier MUST NOT draw from a receipt issued under this act type, for example that the receipt does not prove regulator acceptance or does not transfer legal liability. The schema constrains shape only: each value is a lowercase snake_case identifier. Recommended identifiers and naming rules are governed in spec/vocabularies.md, so a new regulation adds entry-specific non-claims as catalogue data without a schema change. Optional. Where present, the values SHOULD also be rendered in human-facing receipts and verification pages.

reliance_context.description: Names who relies on receipts under this act type, what decision they make, who later verifies independently, and what the receipt does not prove. Optional. Recommended for any act type whose value depends on third-party reliance. Introduced in v3.

public_fields.description: Field references stored cleartext in the canonical manifest. Field references MAY be simple claim field names (e.g. 'supplier_name') or dotted manifest paths (e.g. 'manifest.title', 'manifest.issuer.legal_name', 'manifest.evidence[].label', 'manifest.recipients[].org_name'). Fields not named in any of the three tier lists default to public.

commitment_fields.description: Field references stored as salted SHA-256 commitments in the canonical manifest. Cleartext lives only in the issuer's holder receipt. Commitment construction is sha256(salt || ':' || qualified_field_name || ':' || canonical_value). Empty array permitted.

private_fields.description: Field references omitted from the manifest entirely. Present only in the issuer's holder receipt. Catalogue releases MAY restrict the use of this tier (e.g. actproof-events v1.5-rc1 entries MUST have private_fields = []). Empty array permitted.

back_propagation_scope.description: Maps a prior_receipts role to the list of field references visible to that prior receipt's issuer in the automatic disclosure receipt generated at settlement. Empty mapping or absent block means no automatic back-propagation. Multiple roles MAY be declared even if a given catalogue release exercises only one.

disclosure_profile.description: Per-field disclosure tier declarations for receipts issued under this act type, plus back-propagation scope for bilateral lifecycle. Optional at the schema level. Catalogue releases MAY require it for all entries. Introduced in v3.

required.description: Whether at least one recognised submission evidence artifact MUST be referenced by a receipt issued under this act type.

supports.description: Evidence label identifiers for submission, transmission, channel, delivery, or authority-reference artifacts the act type recognises. These are distinct from signature_policy.supports: a submission acknowledgement is evidence that a filing reached its recipient, not a signature over the filing's content.

submission_evidence_policy.description: Submission and transmission evidence policy for this act type. Declares whether submission evidence is required and which submission, acknowledgement, channel, or authority-reference artifacts the act type recognises. Distinct from signature_policy, which governs signature and attestation artifacts over the act's own content. Optional. Introduced as an additive revision of v3.

claim_field_types.description: Maps each claim field identifier to its primitive data type, so a catalogue-driven consumer can render the input control, parse the submitted value, and serialise the manifest with no hardcoded per-act-type field knowledge. Keys are claim field identifiers and SHOULD cover every member of required_claim_fields and optional_claim_fields. A consumer encountering a claim field absent from this map SHOULD treat it as 'string'. Type meanings: 'string' is a short single-line value; 'text' is a long multi-line value; 'boolean' is true or false; 'integer' is a whole number; 'number' is a decimal number; 'date' is an ISO 8601 calendar date (YYYY-MM-DD); 'datetime' is an ISO 8601 timestamp in UTC; 'email' is an email address; 'string_list' is an ordered list of strings, that is a multi-value field. Optional at the schema level. A catalogue release MAY require it for all entries surfaced for new issuance. Introduced as an additive revision of v3.

source_bindings.description: The official legal instruments this profile is built from, each pinned by the SHA-256 hash of a retrieved official artefact. This block is the trust anchor of the profile. It lets anyone independently fetch the cited sources, reproduce the hashes, and audit the claim schema against the official text, with no AI and no cost. There is one entry per instrument; the provisions the profile relies on are listed inside the entry. The block is jurisdiction-agnostic: source_type names the official-source adapter and identifiers carries whatever citation schemes that jurisdiction uses. Optional at the schema level so that entries authored before source binding remain valid. The authoring process requires it for any newly authored profile.

source_binding_id.description: Stable identifier for this source binding, unique within the entry. Lets profiles, interpretation links, and field derivations reference a specific binding by a name that does not change when the binding list is reordered. Optional so entries authored before stable ids remain valid. Example: 'sb_dora_reg_2022_2554'.

source_type.description: Identifier of the official-source adapter the binding was retrieved through. 'eurlex' is the European Union adapter, resolving CELEX and ELI identifiers through the EU Publications Office. A United States profile would use a different value, for example 'us_ecfr'. The standard does not assume any one jurisdiction.

authority.description: The body that adopted the instrument. Examples: 'European Commission'; 'European Parliament and Council of the European Union'.

instrument.description: Human-readable name of the legal instrument. Example: 'Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2025/301'. Naming MUST be precise: a regulation is not a directive, and the instrument type is part of its name.

identifiers.description: Citation identifiers for the instrument, as a scheme-to-value map. Open by design, so a jurisdiction adds its own schemes with no schema change. For an EU instrument the schemes are 'celex' (for example '32025R0301') and 'eli' (the ELI URI). At least one identifier MUST be present, and each MUST be a stable, resolvable identifier in a published scheme.

provisions.description: The specific provisions of this instrument that the profile's claim schema relies on. Examples: ['Article 2']; ['Annex I', 'Annex II']. These are the parts a reviewer reads against the claim schema when performing the open audit.

artifact_type.description: The kind of official artefact that was retrieved and hashed. 'oj_pdf' is the Official Journal PDF, the authentic published form of an EU legal act.

media_type.description: IANA media type of the retrieved artefact. Example: 'application/pdf'.

sha256.description: SHA-256 of the retrieved artefact's bytes, prefixed with 'sha256:'. A verifier re-fetching the cited source reproduces this value. A later mismatch means the instrument may have been amended and the profile must be revisited.

retrieved_at.description: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp at which the artefact was retrieved and hashed.

url.description: The exact URL the artefact was retrieved from, so the retrieval is reproducible.

method.description: The retrieval strategy used, recorded for build-time provenance. Examples: 'cellar-content-negotiation'; 'eurlex-direct-pdf'.

retrieved_from.description: Where and how the artefact was retrieved.

artifact.description: The official artefact retrieved and hashed for this instrument. The profile binds by reference: it cites and hashes the source, it does not copy the source text into the catalogue.

method.description: How the profile's claim schema was produced. 'ai_assisted_extraction_from_authoritative_sources' means a language model drafted the schema by reading the hashed official sources, and the draft was then reconciled against those sources. 'manual_authoring' means a person authored the schema directly. The method does not affect trust: every profile is audited against its hashed source regardless of how it was drafted.

extraction_tool.description: For an ai-assisted profile, the model or tool that produced the draft. Recorded for transparency only. The extraction step is model-agnostic and sits outside the trust path, so this value is provenance, not a trust input. Omitted for a manually authored profile.

reconciled.description: Whether the draft schema was checked, field by field, against the hashed official text before publication. This is the Stage 4 reconciliation of the authoring process. It SHOULD be true for any published profile.

reconciliation_note.description: Short account of how reconciliation was performed and by whom or what, for example a human review, a second-model cross-check, or both. The full account belongs in the transparency note.

authoring_process.description: Name or reference of the authoring process the profile was produced under. Example: 'actproof-events authoring process, eight-stage'.

generated_at.description: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp at which the profile was assembled.

generation.description: Machine-readable record of how this profile was produced. The transparency note referenced by transparency_note_reference is the human-readable companion to this block, and the two MUST agree. Optional at the schema level so that entries authored before this block remain valid. The authoring process requires it for any newly authored profile.

transparency_note_reference.description: Repository-relative path to the human-readable transparency note for this profile. The note is the prose companion to the generation block. It states the source as concrete hashes, what the law prescribes in plain language, the data-model choices and where authorial judgement entered, and an open invitation to compare the profile against its cited sources and contribute. A profile is not complete without it. Optional at the schema level for backward compatibility. The authoring process requires it for any newly authored profile.

maturity.description: Maturity of this profile, declared so that a consumer can decide whether to rely on it without reading prose. 'draft': the profile is illustrative or early, and its claim schema, required fields, or source bindings may change without notice; not for production receipts. 'candidate': the profile is source-bound and reconciled against its hashed official sources and is believed correct, but is still gathering validation and may change before it stabilises; suitable for evaluation and pilot use. 'stable': the claim schema and required fields are settled, and any further change follows the schema versioning policy; safe to depend on. 'deprecated': the profile is superseded or withdrawn, and the transparency note or a successor entry explains the replacement; not for new receipts.

since.description: The actproof-events specification version at which the profile reached its current maturity, for example '1.5-rc1'. Recorded so that a consumer can see how long the current status has held. Optional.

summary.description: Short human-readable note on the profile's status: in one or two sentences, why it sits at this maturity and what would move it forward. For a source-bound profile, the full account belongs in the transparency note. Optional.

profile_status.description: Machine-readable maturity declaration for this profile. It lets a consumer decide, without reading prose, whether the profile is safe to rely on. Optional at the schema level so that entries authored before this block remain valid. A newly authored or revised profile SHOULD declare it. Absence is equivalent to no maturity claim, which a cautious consumer should treat as no stronger than 'draft'.
